steps.title: Match data to the hypothesis
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues predicted that paired grapevine leaves would tend to show opposite-side orientation. Supporting data should therefore show opposite-side orientation occurring more often than same-side orientation across the tested species, not merely one species with a high opposite-side count.

steps.title: Reject partial graph readings
steps.content: Choice A focuses on same-side counts and compares species to each other, but it does not show that opposite-side orientation is more common than same-side orientation in each species. Choice B goes too far because the graph still shows some same-side pairs. Choice D discusses July grape and graybark grape only, leaving out the full pattern. The relevant support is that graybark grape, Amur grape, and July grape all have more opposite-side pairs than same-side pairs, even though the exact ratios differ. The answer is C.
